metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = tunneling current and exponential decay tail; CRIMSON = classical expectation (gradual linear change)
metadata.note: Exclusions: no kappa derivation from WKB, no STM feedback electronics, no surface reconstruction physics, no scanning-mode details

beats.narration_text: A scanning tunneling microscope maps individual atoms. Move the tip one ten-billionth of a meter closer. The current jumps by a factor of seven.

card.headline: Why Moving a Tip 1 Angstrom Closer Changes Tunneling Current by 7

beats.narration_text: Binnig and Rohrer at IBM Zurich in 1981: a sharp metal tip held 5 angstroms above silicon. Lower it 1 angstrom. Current multiplies by roughly 7. Raise it 1 angstrom. Current divides by 7. That factor is how they saw single atoms.
Moving a conductor closer should gradually increase current — a smooth, roughly linear change. Here a step smaller than an atom multiplies current by seven. Why so dramatic?

card.headline: Moving a conductor closer should gradually increase current. Here a 1-angstrom step multiplies current by 7. Why does a change smaller than an atom produce such a dramatic response?

beats.narration_text: Classical reasoning: current depends on how many electrons can cross the gap. Halving the gap roughly doubles the current. A one-angstrom step is small — surely a small change gives a small result.
But these electrons do not cross the gap. The gap is a vacuum barrier the electrons cannot classically cross. They tunnel — and the tunneling probability falls exponentially with barrier width.
The exponential is doing the work, not the distance.

card.headline:
Tunneling decays as
e to the minus 2 kappa d

beats.narration_text: The wavefunction leaks into the vacuum barrier as an exponential decay. The probability of finding the electron on the far side falls as e to the minus 2 kappa d, where d is the gap width and kappa is about 1 per angstrom for typical metals. Each angstrom of extra gap multiplies current by e squared — about 7.4.

This means the current does not grow linearly as the tip approaches. On a log scale, current versus distance is a straight line with slope minus 2 kappa. That linearity — on a log scale — is the signature of the exponential.
And that exponential is what sees atoms.

An atom protruding 1 angstrom above its neighbors is 7 times closer to the tip than the flat surface. So it carries 7 times more current. The microscope does not image atoms directly — the exponential amplifies the height difference into a current ratio large enough to measure.

props.headline: An atom protruding 1 angstrom above its neighbors is 7 times…

beats.narration_text: Without the exponential decay of the wavefunction, no tip would ever resolve a single atom. The entire resolution mechanism is quantum — tunneling probability amplified by the exponential.

Work function 4 eV gives kappa of 1.02 per angstrom. Gap of 5 angstroms: current I-naught. Gap of 6 angstroms: current I-naught divided by 7.4. Gap of 4 angstroms: current 7.4 times I-naught. An atom sticking 1 angstrom above its neighbors carries 7 times more current than the flat surface beside it. That atom is now visible.

A step smaller than an atom multiplies current by 7 because the tunneling probability decays as an exponential. Linear changes in distance produce exponential changes in current. That is why the STM sees atoms.

card.headline: Each angstrom of gap multiplies current by e-squared ~7. The exponential of quantum tunneling gives the STM its atomic resolution.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics A Companion Guide series.
